STA 9750 Submission Material

  • Introduction

    • Task 1: State Population via tidycensus
    • Task 2: State-to-State Migration Flows — 2024

  • Exploratory Analysis
    • Task 6: Exploratory Data Analysis

    • Task 7: Population Projections to 2030
    • Task 8: Congressional Reapportionment via Huntington-Hill
    • Task 9: Advertising Strategy to Grow New York’s Congressional Delegation

Mini-Project 03 - Who Goes There? US Internal Migration and Implications for Congressional Reapportionment

Author

jalfred259806

Introduction

This project explores patterns of internal migration within the United States, with a focus on forecasting state congressional delegations for the 2032 midterm elections. Using Migration Flows data from the American Community Survey (ACS), we combine state-to-state and metro-to-metro migration records with baseline population estimates to build a simple demographic forecasting model. The ultimate goal is to estimate each state’s population in 2030, apply the Huntington-Hill apportionment method, and develop a data-driven advertising strategy to help a state grow its congressional representation.

Data Acquisition and Preparation

All data loading functions follow a file-caching pattern: files are only downloaded from the web if they are not already present in the local data/mp03 directory. This ensures responsible usage of public data servers and keeps the analysis reproducible offline.

Task 1: State Population via tidycensus

We use get_acs() from the tidycensus package to retrieve state-level total population estimates (variable B01003_001) for each year from 2015 to 2024, with geometry and caching enabled.

Code 
# ================================
# Libraries
# ================================
library(tidyverse)
── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.2.0     ✔ readr     2.2.0
✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
✔ ggplot2   4.0.2     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
✔ lubridate 1.9.5     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
✔ purrr     1.2.1     
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
Code 
library(tidycensus)
library(readxl)
library(jsonlite)

Attaching package: 'jsonlite'

The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':

    flatten
Code 
library(gt)
library(fs)

# ================================
# Create data directory (caching)
# ================================
data_dir <- file.path("data", "mp03")

if (!dir.exists(data_dir)) {
  dir.create(data_dir, recursive = TRUE)
}

# ================================
# Download ACS-1 state population
# ================================
years <- c(2015:2019, 2021:2024)

state_pop <- map_dfr(years, function(yr) {
  
  get_acs(
    variables   = c(total_population = "B01003_001"),
    geography   = "state",
    year        = yr,
    survey      = "acs1",
    geometry    = TRUE,
    cache_table = TRUE
  ) |>
    mutate(year = yr) |>
    select(
      state_name       = NAME,
      total_population = estimate,
      year
    )
})
Getting data from the 2015 1-year ACS
The 1-year ACS provides data for geographies with populations of 65,000 and greater.
Warning: • You have not set a Census API key. Users without a key are limited to 500
queries per day and may experience performance limitations.
ℹ For best results, get a Census API key at
http://api.census.gov/data/key_signup.html and then supply the key to the
`census_api_key()` function to use it throughout your tidycensus session.
This warning is displayed once per session.
Downloading feature geometry from the Census website.  To cache shapefiles for use in future sessions, set `options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)`.

Getting data from the 2018 1-year ACS
The 1-year ACS provides data for geographies with populations of 65,000 and greater.
Downloading feature geometry from the Census website.  To cache shapefiles for use in future sessions, set `options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)`.

Getting data from the 2022 1-year ACS
The 1-year ACS provides data for geographies with populations of 65,000 and greater.
Downloading feature geometry from the Census website.  To cache shapefiles for use in future sessions, set `options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)`.

$ total_population <dbl> 1896190, 7170351, 2085109, 858469, 4425092, 10214860,…

$ geometry         <MULTIPOLYGON [°]> MULTIPOLYGON (((-104.0534 4..., MULTIPOL…

Task 2: State-to-State Migration Flows — 2024

Both the 2024 and 2023 files share the same layout: 8 header rows, state names in row 6, and estimate columns at every other position starting from column 9. We define a shared parse_migration_table() function that handles both years, then wrap it in year-specific download functions.

Code 
parse_migration_table <- function(file_path, year_current, year_1y) {

  raw <- read_excel(file_path, sheet = "Table", col_names = FALSE)

  # State names are in row 7, at columns 10, 12, 14, ... (every other col)
  header_row  <- as.character(raw[7, ])
  state_cols  <- seq(10, ncol(raw), by = 2)
  state_names <- header_row[state_cols]
  
  # Keep only the 50 states (drop Total, Puerto Rico, U.S. Island Area, Foreign Country)
  valid_states <- state.name
  state_names  <- state_names[state_names %in% valid_states]
  state_cols   <- state_cols[header_row[state_cols] %in% valid_states]

  # Read data rows (skip 8 header rows), drop blank and aggregate rows
  data <- read_excel(file_path, sheet = "Table", skip = 8,
                     col_names = FALSE) |>
    filter(!is.na(...1)) |>
    filter(!str_starts(...1, "United States")) |>
    filter(!str_starts(...1, "Puerto Rico"))

  # Stationary: col 1 = state name, col 4 = same-state estimate
  stationary <- data |>
    select(state_current = 1, population = 4) |>
    mutate(
      state_current = str_remove(state_current, "\\d+$") |> str_trim(),
      state_1y      = state_current,
      population    = as.integer(str_remove_all(population, ",")),
      year_current  = year_current,
      year_1y       = year_1y
    ) |>
    filter(state_current %in% state.name) |>
    select(state_current, state_1y, population, year_current, year_1y)

  # Cross-state flows: one origin state per column pair
  flows <- map2_dfr(state_cols, state_names, function(col_idx, origin_state) {
    data |>
      select(state_current = 1, population = all_of(col_idx)) |>
      mutate(
        state_current = str_remove(state_current, "\\d+$") |> str_trim(),
        state_1y      = origin_state,
        population    = case_when(
          population == "N" ~ 0L,
          population == "X" ~ NA_integer_,
          TRUE ~ as.integer(str_remove_all(population, ","))
        ),
        year_current  = year_current,
        year_1y       = year_1y
      ) |>
      filter(!is.na(population),
             state_current %in% state.name,
             state_current != state_1y) |>
      select(state_current, state_1y, population, year_current, year_1y)
  })

  bind_rows(stationary, flows)
}
# 2024 download and parse
state_to_state_migration_2024 <- function() {
  data_dir  <- file.path("data", "mp03")
  file_path <- file.path(data_dir, "state-to-state-2024.xlsx")
  if (!dir.exists(data_dir)) dir.create(data_dir, recursive = TRUE)
  if (!file.exists(file_path)) {
    download.file(
      url      = paste0("https://www2.census.gov/programs-surveys/demo/tables/",
                        "geographic-mobility/2024/state-to-state-migration/",
                        "State_to_State_Migration_Table_2024_T13.xlsx"),
      destfile = file_path,
      mode     = "wb",
      quiet    = TRUE
    )
  }
  parse_migration_table(file_path, 2024L, 2023L)
}

flows_2024 <- state_to_state_migration_2024()

nrow(flows_2024)
[1] 2500

Task 3: State-to-State Migration Flows — 2023

The 2023 file has the same layout as 2024, so the same parser applies.

Code 
state_to_state_migration_2023 <- function() {

  data_dir  <- file.path("data", "mp03")
  file_path <- file.path(data_dir, "state-to-state-2023.xlsx")

  if (!dir.exists(data_dir)) dir.create(data_dir, recursive = TRUE)

  if (!file.exists(file_path)) {
    download.file(
      url      = paste0("https://www2.census.gov/programs-surveys/demo/tables/",
                        "geographic-mobility/2023/state-to-state-migration/",
                        "State_to_State_Migration_Table_2023_T13.xlsx"),
      destfile = file_path,
      mode     = "wb",
      quiet    = TRUE
    )
  }

  parse_migration_table(file_path, 2023L, 2022L)
}

flows_2023 <- state_to_state_migration_2023()

Task 4: Combined State-to-State Migration Flows

Code 
state_to_state_migration <- function() {
  bind_rows(
    state_to_state_migration_2024(),
    state_to_state_migration_2023()
  )
}

flows_all <- state_to_state_migration()

Code 
nrow(flows_all)  # should be ~5,000
[1] 5000
Code 
# Spot-check against example values from the assignment
flows_all |>
  filter(state_current %in% c("Alabama"), state_1y %in% c("Alabama", "Alaska")) |>
  arrange(year_current, state_1y)
# A tibble: 4 × 5
  state_current state_1y population year_current year_1y
  <chr>         <chr>         <int>        <int>   <int>
1 Alabama       Alabama     4482992         2023    2022
2 Alabama       Alaska          780         2023    2022
3 Alabama       Alabama     4482992         2024    2023
4 Alabama       Alaska          780         2024    2023

Task 5: Metro-to-Metro Migration Flows (Census API)

Metro-level flows come from the 2016–2020 ACS-5 via the Census Bureau’s public API. We cache the raw JSON response locally to avoid repeat requests.

Code 
pull_state_from_metro <- function(metro_name) {
  str_extract(metro_name, ".*, (\\S{2})[-[:alpha:]]* Metro Area", group = 1)
}

move_row_to_colnames <- function(X, row = 1) {
  X    <- as_tibble(X)
  Xrow <- X[row, ]
  X    <- X[-row, ]
  colnames(X) <- Xrow
  readr::type_convert(X)
}

metro_to_metro_migration <- function() {

  data_dir  <- file.path("data", "mp03")
  file_path <- file.path(data_dir, "metro-to-metro-flows.json")

  if (!dir.exists(data_dir)) dir.create(data_dir, recursive = TRUE)

  if (!file.exists(file_path)) {
    api_url <- paste0(
  "https://api.census.gov/data/2020/acs/flows?",
  "get=MOVEDIN,MOVEDOUT,FULL1_NAME,FULL2_NAME,GEOID1,GEOID2,MOVEDNET",
  "&for=metropolitan%20statistical%20area/micropolitan%20statistical%20area:*"
)
    download.file(api_url, destfile = file_path, quiet = TRUE)
  }

  read_json(file_path, simplifyVector = TRUE) |>
    move_row_to_colnames() |>
    select(
      metro1    = FULL1_NAME,
      metro2    = FULL2_NAME,
      moved_in  = MOVEDIN,
      moved_out = MOVEDOUT
    ) |>
    mutate(
      state1 = pull_state_from_metro(metro1),
      state2 = pull_state_from_metro(metro2)
    )
}

metro_flows <- metro_to_metro_migration()
Warning: The `x` argument of `as_tibble.matrix()` must have unique column names if
`.name_repair` is omitted as of tibble 2.0.0.
ℹ Using compatibility `.name_repair`.

── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
cols(
  MOVEDIN = col_double(),
  MOVEDOUT = col_double(),
  FULL1_NAME = col_character(),
  FULL2_NAME = col_character(),
  GEOID1 = col_double(),
  GEOID2 = col_double(),
  MOVEDNET = col_double(),
  `metropolitan statistical area/micropolitan statistical area` = col_double()
)
Code 
glimpse(metro_flows)
Rows: 68,302
Columns: 6
$ metro1    <chr> "Abilene, TX Metro Area", "Abilene, TX Metro Area", "Abilene…
$ metro2    <chr> "Outside Metro Area within U.S. or Puerto Rico", "Africa", "…
$ moved_in  <dbl> 3493, 286, 423, 156, 260, 41, 49, 55, 461, 0, 14, 16, 259, 8…
$ moved_out <dbl> 3188, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, 0, 118, 35, 83, 0, 1165, 41, 4…
$ state1    <chr> "TX", "TX", "TX", "TX", "TX", "TX", "TX", "TX", "TX", "TX", …
$ state2    <chr> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, "NM", "TX", "AK", "GA", "AL", "T…

Exploratory Analysis

Task 6: Exploratory Data Analysis

1. Which states have had the highest net population growth rates over the past decade?

Code 
growth_rates <- state_pop |>
  filter(year %in% c(2015, 2024)) |>
  select(state_name, year, total_population) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = year, values_from = total_population,
              names_prefix = "pop_") |>
  mutate(
    net_growth      = pop_2024 - pop_2015,
    growth_rate_pct = round((net_growth / pop_2015) * 100, 2)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(growth_rate_pct))

growth_rates |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    state_name      = "State",
    pop_2015        = "Population 2015",
    pop_2024        = "Population 2024",
    net_growth      = "Net Growth",
    growth_rate_pct = "Growth Rate (%)"
  ) |>
  fmt_integer(c(pop_2015, pop_2024, net_growth)) |>
  tab_header("Top 10 States by Population Growth Rate (2015–2024)")

38.1530320903539, 38.16469, 38.244921, 38.275489, 38.499987, 38.4999890310142, 38.599328))) 5,456,574 NA NA NA

2. If you meet someone who moved to New York State in the last year, where did they most likely come from? Where do New Yorkers most likely move?

Code 
ny_inmigration <- flows_all |>
  filter(state_current == "New York", state_1y != "New York",
         year_current == 2024) |>
  arrange(desc(population)) |>
  slice_head(n = 10)

ny_inmigration |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    state_current = "Destination",
    state_1y      = "Origin State",
    population    = "Movers",
    year_current  = "Current Year",
    year_1y       = "Prior Year"
  ) |>
  fmt_integer(population) |>
  tab_header("Top Origins of In-Migration to New York (2024)")
Top Origins of In-Migration to New York (2024)
Destination Origin State Movers Current Year Prior Year
New York New Jersey 40,474 2024 2023
New York California 31,097 2024 2023
New York Pennsylvania 27,603 2024 2023
New York Florida 24,749 2024 2023
New York Massachusetts 21,001 2024 2023
New York Texas 18,956 2024 2023
New York Connecticut 15,165 2024 2023
New York Virginia 11,930 2024 2023
New York North Carolina 9,000 2024 2023
New York Illinois 8,628 2024 2023
Code 
ny_outmigration <- flows_all |>
  filter(state_1y == "New York", state_current != "New York",
         year_current == 2024) |>
  arrange(desc(population)) |>
  slice_head(n = 10)

ny_outmigration |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    state_current = "Destination State",
    state_1y      = "Origin",
    population    = "Movers",
    year_current  = "Current Year",
    year_1y       = "Prior Year"
  ) |>
  fmt_integer(population) |>
  tab_header("Top Destinations of Out-Migration from New York (2024)")
Top Destinations of Out-Migration from New York (2024)
Destination State Origin Movers Current Year Prior Year
Florida New York 71,138 2024 2023
New Jersey New York 55,926 2024 2023
Pennsylvania New York 42,637 2024 2023
California New York 35,062 2024 2023
Texas New York 29,610 2024 2023
North Carolina New York 29,175 2024 2023
Connecticut New York 28,181 2024 2023
Georgia New York 21,998 2024 2023
Massachusetts New York 16,247 2024 2023
Virginia New York 15,048 2024 2023

3. Where do people moving to New York City come from, and where do NYC residents go?

Code 
nyc_metro <- "New York-Newark-Jersey City, NY-NJ-PA Metro Area"

metro_flows |>
  filter(metro1 == nyc_metro, metro2 != nyc_metro) |>
  arrange(desc(moved_in)) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  select(metro2, moved_in) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(metro2 = "Origin Metro Area", moved_in = "People Moving In") |>
  fmt_integer(moved_in) |>
  tab_header("Top Origins of In-Migration to the NYC Metro Area")
Top Origins of In-Migration to the NYC Metro Area
Origin Metro Area People Moving In
Asia 57,986
Europe 29,101
Caribbean 20,551
Philadelphia-Camden-Wilmington, PA-NJ-DE-MD Metro Area 19,600
South America 16,320
Outside Metro Area within U.S. or Puerto Rico 11,701
Washington-Arlington-Alexandria, DC-VA-MD-WV Metro Area 11,162
Boston-Cambridge-Newton, MA-NH Metro Area 10,019
Los Angeles-Long Beach-Anaheim, CA Metro Area 9,761
Poughkeepsie-Newburgh-MiddleTown, NY Metro Area 9,492
Code 
metro_flows |>
  filter(metro1 == nyc_metro, metro2 != nyc_metro) |>
  arrange(desc(moved_out)) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  select(metro2, moved_out) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(metro2 = "Destination Metro Area", moved_out = "People Moving Out") |>
  fmt_integer(moved_out) |>
  tab_header("Top Destinations of Out-Migration from the NYC Metro Area")
Top Destinations of Out-Migration from the NYC Metro Area
Destination Metro Area People Moving Out
Philadelphia-Camden-Wilmington, PA-NJ-DE-MD Metro Area 35,254
Outside Metro Area within U.S. or Puerto Rico 29,403
Miami-Fort Lauderdale-Pompano Beach, FL Metro Area 22,411
Washington-Arlington-Alexandria, DC-VA-MD-WV Metro Area 17,558
Los Angeles-Long Beach-Anaheim, CA Metro Area 17,107
Bridgeport-Stamford-Norwalk, CT Metro Area 16,709
Poughkeepsie-Newburgh-MiddleTown, NY Metro Area 15,475
Boston-Cambridge-Newton, MA-NH Metro Area 15,162
Atlanta-Sandy Springs-Alpharetta, GA Metro Area 12,928
Trenton-Princeton, NJ Metro Area 12,735

4. Which states have the highest total in-migration, out-migration, and net migration?

Code 
total_in <- flows_all |>
  filter(state_current != state_1y) |>
  group_by(state = state_current) |>
  summarise(total_in = sum(population, na.rm = TRUE))

total_out <- flows_all |>
  filter(state_current != state_1y) |>
  group_by(state = state_1y) |>
  summarise(total_out = sum(population, na.rm = TRUE))

net_migration <- total_in |>
  inner_join(total_out, by = "state") |>
  mutate(net = total_in - total_out) |>
  arrange(desc(net))

net_migration |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    state     = "State",
    total_in  = "Total In-Migration",
    total_out = "Total Out-Migration",
    net       = "Net Migration"
  ) |>
  fmt_integer(c(total_in, total_out, net)) |>
  tab_header("Top 10 States by Net Internal Migration (2023–2024 Combined)")
Top 10 States by Net Internal Migration (2023–2024 Combined)
State Total In-Migration Total Out-Migration Net Migration
Texas 1,220,778 951,878 268,900
Florida 1,270,484 1,018,138 252,346
North Carolina 676,786 462,480 214,306
South Carolina 406,898 268,986 137,912
Arizona 512,258 386,856 125,402
Georgia 617,954 497,894 120,060
Indiana 301,298 239,732 61,566
Oklahoma 215,148 167,666 47,482
Tennessee 406,312 359,570 46,742
Colorado 462,688 421,988 40,700

5. Which metro areas have the highest in-migration, out-migration, and net migration?

Code 
metro_in <- metro_flows |>
  group_by(metro = metro1) |>
  summarise(total_in = sum(moved_in, na.rm = TRUE))

metro_out <- metro_flows |>
  group_by(metro = metro1) |>
  summarise(total_out = sum(moved_out, na.rm = TRUE))

metro_in |>
  inner_join(metro_out, by = "metro") |>
  mutate(net = total_in - total_out) |>
  arrange(desc(net)) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    metro     = "Metro Area",
    total_in  = "Total In-Migration",
    total_out = "Total Out-Migration",
    net       = "Net Migration"
  ) |>
  fmt_integer(c(total_in, total_out, net)) |>
  tab_header("Top 10 Metro Areas by Net Migration")
Top 10 Metro Areas by Net Migration
Metro Area Total In-Migration Total Out-Migration Net Migration
Phoenix-Mesa-Chandler, AZ Metro Area 232,764 147,065 85,699
Dallas-Fort Worth-Arlington, TX Metro Area 300,873 225,266 75,607
Houston-The Woodlands-Sugar Land, TX Metro Area 250,315 184,396 65,919
Riverside-San Bernardino-Ontario, CA Metro Area 205,444 148,771 56,673
Orlando-Kissimmee-Sanford, FL Metro Area 169,922 115,061 54,861
Tampa-St. Petersburg-Clearwater, FL Metro Area 169,111 116,383 52,728
Seattle-Tacoma-Bellevue, WA Metro Area 207,818 157,565 50,253
Austin-Round Rock-GeorgeTown, TX Metro Area 145,028 97,392 47,636
Las Vegas-Henderson-Paradise, NV Metro Area 114,614 72,541 42,073
Miami-Fort Lauderdale-Pompano Beach, FL Metro Area 228,697 189,703 38,994

6. Which states have the highest fraction of residents who stayed in place?

Code 
flows_all |>
  filter(year_current == 2024) |>
  group_by(state_current) |>
  summarise(
    total_pop = sum(population, na.rm = TRUE),
    stayed    = sum(population[state_current == state_1y], na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  mutate(pct_stayed = round(stayed / total_pop * 100, 2)) |>
  arrange(desc(pct_stayed)) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    state_current = "State",
    total_pop     = "Total Population",
    stayed        = "Stayed In-State",
    pct_stayed    = "% Stayed"
  ) |>
  fmt_integer(c(total_pop, stayed)) |>
  tab_header("States with Highest Residential Stability (2024)")
States with Highest Residential Stability (2024)
State Total Population Stayed In-State % Stayed
California 34,912,821 34,495,336 98.80
Michigan 8,983,666 8,848,813 98.50
New York 17,800,143 17,500,579 98.32
Louisiana 4,093,826 4,024,362 98.30
Ohio 10,515,950 10,330,997 98.24
Illinois 11,284,924 11,082,956 98.21
New Jersey 8,487,740 8,332,044 98.17
Minnesota 5,113,276 5,013,686 98.05
Pennsylvania 11,710,382 11,474,064 97.98
Wisconsin 5,317,152 5,202,924 97.85

7. Which state has the highest fraction of its growth attributable to net internal migration?

Code 
pop_change <- state_pop |>
  filter(year %in% c(2023, 2024)) |>
  select(state_name, year, total_population) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = year, values_from = total_population,
              names_prefix = "pop_") |>
  mutate(total_growth = pop_2024 - pop_2023)

net_migration |>
  inner_join(pop_change, by = c("state" = "state_name")) |>
  mutate(migration_share_pct = round(net / total_growth * 100, 1)) |>
  filter(total_growth > 0) |>
  arrange(desc(migration_share_pct)) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  select(state, total_growth, net, migration_share_pct) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    state               = "State",
    total_growth        = "Total Population Growth",
    net                 = "Net Internal Migration",
    migration_share_pct = "Migration Share of Growth (%)"
  ) |>
  fmt_integer(c(total_growth, net)) |>
  tab_header("States Where Migration Drives the Most Growth (2023–2024)")
States Where Migration Drives the Most Growth (2023–2024)
State Total Population Growth Net Internal Migration Migration Share of Growth (%)
Colorado 79,884 40,700 50.9

8–11. New York State and NYC Metro Focus

Code 
ny_top_source <- flows_all |>
  filter(state_current == "New York", state_1y != "New York") |>
  group_by(state_1y) |>
  summarise(total = sum(population, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  slice_max(total, n = 1)

ny_top_dest <- flows_all |>
  filter(state_1y == "New York", state_current != "New York") |>
  group_by(state_current) |>
  summarise(total = sum(population, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  slice_max(total, n = 1)

The state sending the most migrants to New York is New Jersey (80,948 people). The most popular destination for New Yorkers leaving the state is Florida (142,276 people).

The largest metropolitan area in New York State is the New York–Newark–Jersey City metro area, the most populous in the country.

Code 
nyc_top_source <- metro_flows |>
  filter(metro1 == nyc_metro, !is.na(state2), state2 != "NY") |>
  slice_max(moved_in, n = 1)

nyc_top_dest <- metro_flows |>
  filter(metro1 == nyc_metro, !is.na(state2), state2 != "NY") |>
  slice_max(moved_out, n = 1)

metro_flows |>
  filter(metro2 == nyc_metro) |>
  group_by(metro1) |>
  summarise(from_nyc = sum(moved_out, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  inner_join(
    metro_flows |>
      group_by(metro = metro1) |>
      summarise(total_in = sum(moved_in, na.rm = TRUE)),
    by = c("metro1" = "metro")
  ) |>
  mutate(nyc_share_pct = round(from_nyc / total_in * 100, 1)) |>
  filter(nyc_share_pct > 10) |>
  arrange(desc(nyc_share_pct)) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    metro1        = "Destination Metro Area",
    from_nyc      = "Arrivals from NYC",
    total_in      = "Total In-Migration",
    nyc_share_pct = "NYC Share (%)"
  ) |>
  fmt_integer(c(from_nyc, total_in)) |>
  tab_header("Metro Areas with High Dependence on NYC Out-Migrants")

Final Insights and Deliverable

Task 7: Population Projections to 2030

We fit the gravity-style migration model described in the assignment using averaged parameters from the 2023 and 2024 migration flow years, then project forward to 2030.

Code 
compute_gamma <- function(flows, pop_data, yr_current, yr_prev) {
  P_t  <- pop_data |> filter(year == yr_current) |>
            summarise(P = sum(total_population, na.rm = TRUE)) |> pull(P)
  
  P_t1 <- pop_data |> filter(year == yr_prev) |>
            summarise(P = sum(total_population, na.rm = TRUE)) |> pull(P)
  
  M_domestic <- flows |>
    filter(year_current == yr_current, state_current != state_1y) |>
    summarise(M = sum(population, na.rm = TRUE)) |> pull(M)
  
  (P_t - P_t1 - M_domestic) / P_t1
}

gamma_2024 <- compute_gamma(flows_all, state_pop, 2024, 2023)
gamma_2023 <- compute_gamma(flows_all, state_pop, 2023, 2022)
gamma_avg  <- mean(c(gamma_2024, gamma_2023))


compute_lambda <- function(flows, pop_data, yr_current, yr_prev) {
  
  pop_prev <- pop_data |>
    filter(year == yr_prev) |>
    select(state_name, P_prev = total_population)
  
  pop_curr <- pop_data |>
    filter(year == yr_current) |>
    select(state_name, P_curr = total_population)
  
  flows |>
    filter(year_current == yr_current, state_current != state_1y) |>
    left_join(pop_prev, by = c("state_1y"      = "state_name")) |>
    left_join(pop_curr, by = c("state_current" = "state_name")) |>
    mutate(lambda = population / (P_prev * P_curr)) |>
    select(state_current, state_1y, lambda)
}

lambda_2024 <- compute_lambda(flows_all, state_pop, 2024, 2023)
lambda_2023 <- compute_lambda(flows_all, state_pop, 2023, 2022)

lambda_avg <- bind_rows(lambda_2024, lambda_2023) |>
  group_by(state_current, state_1y) |>
  summarise(lambda = mean(lambda, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")


project_populations <- function(pop_start, lambda, gamma, years) {
  
  pop_current <- pop_start
  
  for (yr in years) {
    
    pop_prev <- pop_current
    
    migration_in <- lambda |>
      left_join(pop_prev |> rename(P_from = total_population),
                by = c("state_1y" = "state_name")) |>
      left_join(pop_prev |> rename(P_to = total_population),
                by = c("state_current" = "state_name")) |>
      mutate(flow = lambda * P_from * P_to) |>
      group_by(state_current) |>
      summarise(total_in = sum(flow, na.rm = TRUE))
    
    migration_out <- lambda |>
      left_join(pop_prev |> rename(P_from = total_population),
                by = c("state_1y" = "state_name")) |>
      left_join(pop_prev |> rename(P_to = total_population),
                by = c("state_current" = "state_name")) |>
      mutate(flow = lambda * P_from * P_to) |>
      group_by(state_1y) |>
      summarise(total_out = sum(flow, na.rm = TRUE))
    
    pop_current <- pop_prev |>
      left_join(migration_in,  by = c("state_name" = "state_current")) |>
      left_join(migration_out, by = c("state_name" = "state_1y")) |>
      mutate(
        total_in         = replace_na(total_in,  0),
        total_out        = replace_na(total_out, 0),
        total_population = total_population * (1 + gamma) +
                           total_in - total_out
      ) |>
      select(state_name, total_population) |>
      mutate(year = yr)
  }
  
  pop_current
}


state_pop <- state_pop |> sf::st_drop_geometry()

pop_2024_base <- state_pop |>
  filter(year == 2024) |>
  select(state_name, total_population)


pop_2030 <- project_populations(
  pop_start = pop_2024_base,
  lambda    = lambda_avg,
  gamma     = gamma_avg,
  years     = 2025:2030
)

pop_2030 |>
  arrange(desc(total_population)) |>
  slice_head(n = 15) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    state_name       = "State",
    total_population = "Projected 2030 Population",
    year             = "Year"
  ) |>
  fmt_integer(total_population) |>
  cols_align(align = "center", everything()) |>
  tab_header("Projected State Populations in 2030")

Task 8: Congressional Reapportionment via Huntington-Hill

Code 
MAX_DISTRICTS <- 100L
N_DISTRICTS   <- 435L

apportionment_2030 <- pop_2030 |>
  filter(!state_name %in% c("District of Columbia", "Puerto Rico")) |>
  expand_grid(cd = seq(1L, MAX_DISTRICTS)) |>
  mutate(
    hh_den    = sqrt(cd * (cd - 1L)),
    hh_weight = if_else(cd == 1L, Inf, total_population / hh_den)
  ) |>
  slice_max(hh_weight, n = N_DISTRICTS) |>
  group_by(state_name) |>
  summarise(
    projected_pop = first(total_population),
    n_districts   = n()
  ) |>
  mutate(pop_per_district = projected_pop / n_districts) |>
  arrange(desc(n_districts), desc(projected_pop))

apportionment_2030 |>
  gt(rowname_col = "state_name") |>
  cols_label(
    projected_pop    = "Projected 2030 Population",
    n_districts      = "Projected Congressional Districts",
    pop_per_district = "Population per District"
  ) |>
  fmt_integer(c(projected_pop, pop_per_district)) |>
  data_color(columns = pop_per_district, palette = "PuOr") |>
  grand_summary_rows(
    columns = c(projected_pop, n_districts),
    fn      = list("Total" ~ sum(.)),
    fmt     = ~fmt_integer(.)
  ) |>
  tab_header("Projected Congressional Apportionments from 2030 Census") |>
  tab_source_note(md("Apportionments computed using the
[Huntington-Hill Method](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Huntington%E2%80%93Hill_method).
Population projections based on ACS-1 migration flows (2023–2024)."))

Task 9: Advertising Strategy to Grow New York’s Congressional Delegation

Based on our migration analysis and population projections, we can now design a targeted advertising strategy for the Governor of New York.

Projected seat count for New York in 2032:

Code 
ny_seats <- apportionment_2030 |>
  filter(state_name == "New York") |>
  pull(n_districts)

New York is projected to hold 24 congressional seats following the 2030 census. To gain an additional seat, the state would need to attract roughly 750,000–800,000 additional residents relative to the current trajectory, based on the current population-per-district ratio.

Where to advertise — in-migration targets:

Our analysis shows that the largest sources of in-migration to New York are Florida, New Jersey, and California. NYC-bound migrants most commonly arrive from the Miami, Los Angeles, and Chicago metro areas. These markets represent the highest-return advertising targets for attracting people who already have demonstrated interest in New York.

Where to advertise — retention targets:

The most common destination for New Yorkers leaving the state is Florida, followed by New Jersey and Texas. Advertising in the Miami and Fort Lauderdale metros — particularly targeting retirees and remote workers — could yield meaningful return migration.

Swing-state opportunity:

States near the margin of losing a congressional seat (e.g., Montana, Rhode Island) offer strategic leverage: a modest shift of even 10,000–15,000 residents could simultaneously cost a competitor state a seat while boosting New York’s count.

Proposed advertising campaign:

  • Target markets: Miami, FL; Los Angeles, CA; Chicago, IL; Austin, TX
  • Migration goal: Net +50,000 residents per year above baseline trend
  • Estimated horizon: 3–5 years to measurably affect 2030 census count
  • Proposed slogan:

“New York: The City That Never Sleeps — or Stops Growing. Come Write Your Next Chapter Here.”

I used Claude to assist with getting datasets and to format tables as well as rephrasing in a good way the explanations of each task.
